title: Confirmation Bias vs Motivated Reasoning
summary: Both protect a favored conclusion, but confirmation bias narrows the search and motivated reasoning bends the whole evaluation around what the person wants to be true.
commonConfusion: The same case often has both: people search selectively because a preferred answer is emotionally or socially useful.
quickRule: Ask whether the main failure is selective evidence intake or desire-driven evaluation after evidence arrives.

left.label: Confirmation bias
left.core: The search, recall, or weighting process favors information that supports the existing belief.
left.ask: What disconfirming search path has been avoided or underweighted?

right.label: Motivated reasoning
right.core: The conclusion is defended because it serves identity, comfort, status, group belonging, or some other motive.
right.ask: What would become costly, embarrassing, or identity-threatening if the conclusion changed?

diagnosticQuestions: Would the same search pattern appear if the preferred answer had no personal or group value?
Is contrary evidence missing, or is it present but being explained away asymmetrically?
What neutral test would both sides accept before seeing the result?

examples.situation: A manager reads only articles supporting a policy she already prefers.
examples.why: The visible failure is selective evidence gathering.

examples.situation: A fan accepts weak evidence for their team and invents elaborate reasons to reject stronger evidence against it.
examples.why: The evaluation standards shift to protect an identity-linked conclusion.

repairMove: Require one serious disconfirming search path, then state in advance what evidence would change the conclusion.

title: Anchoring Effect vs Framing Effect
summary: Anchoring pulls judgment toward a starting value; framing changes judgment by changing how the same substance is described.
commonConfusion: A first number can also be a frame, especially when it makes later options feel cheap, expensive, risky, or safe.
quickRule: Ask whether the distortion is caused by a starting point or by a presentation shift.

left.label: Anchoring effect
left.core: An initial number, estimate, or reference point drags later judgments toward itself.
left.ask: What estimate would people make before seeing the first number?

right.label: Framing effect
right.core: Different descriptions of equivalent substance produce different judgments.
right.ask: Would the preference survive a neutral wording or the opposite frame?

diagnosticQuestions: Can you remove the first number and still reproduce the shift?
Can you restate the same facts in gain and loss language and watch the preference move?
Is the reference point numerical, narrative, emotional, or all three?

examples.situation: A salary negotiation starts high and every counteroffer stays near that number.
examples.why: The opening number is the active pull.

examples.situation: A policy gains support as '90 lives saved' and loses support as '10 lives lost.'
examples.why: The same outcome changes force when the description changes.

repairMove: Make an independent estimate first, then restate the decision in at least two equivalent frames.

title: Halo Effect vs Fundamental Attribution Error
summary: The halo effect lets one positive impression spill into unrelated judgments; fundamental attribution error overreads behavior as character while underreading situation.
commonConfusion: Both can make a people judgment feel obvious before evidence is strong enough.
quickRule: Ask whether one admired trait is spilling outward, or whether a behavior is being turned into a trait explanation too quickly.

left.label: Halo effect
left.core: One salient good quality raises judgments about other qualities without independent evidence.
left.ask: Which unrelated trait is being inferred from the admired feature?

right.label: Fundamental attribution error
right.core: Behavior is explained by stable character while situational pressures are ignored.
right.ask: What pressures, constraints, incentives, or accidents could explain the behavior?

diagnosticQuestions: Is the judgment moving from one trait to another trait?
Is the judgment moving from one behavior to a character explanation?
What would you think if the same behavior came from a less admired person?

examples.situation: A charismatic speaker is assumed to be technically competent.
examples.why: Warmth and polish are spilling into competence.

examples.situation: A person snaps once in a stressful meeting and is labeled disrespectful.
examples.why: The behavior becomes character before the situation is inspected.

repairMove: Separate observed behavior, inferred trait, and situational context into three explicit columns.

title: Hindsight Bias vs Outcome Bias
summary: Hindsight bias makes the outcome feel predictable after the fact; outcome bias uses the result to grade the earlier decision process.
commonConfusion: Postmortems often slide from 'we should have known' into 'therefore the decision was bad.'
quickRule: Ask whether the claim is about prior predictability or decision quality.

left.label: Hindsight bias
left.core: After knowing the result, people remember the earlier uncertainty as cleaner and more predictable.
left.ask: What did we actually believe before the outcome was known?

right.label: Outcome bias
right.core: The result is used as evidence that the decision process was good or bad.
right.ask: Was the process sound by the evidence available at the time?

diagnosticQuestions: Are people saying the result was obvious, or that the process was proven good or bad?
Do timestamped forecasts contradict the current story?
Would the process get the same grade if luck had gone the other way?

examples.situation: After a vendor fails, people say the warning signs were obvious all along.
examples.why: The known result is making prior uncertainty disappear.

examples.situation: A risky launch is praised as smart because it happened to work.
examples.why: The result is being used to grade the process.

repairMove: Use decision journals: recover prior forecasts first, then grade process separately from outcome.

title: Default Effect vs Status Quo Bias
summary: The default effect is a choice-architecture pull toward the preselected option; status quo bias is a broader preference for leaving things as they are.
commonConfusion: Defaults often create or reinforce the status quo, so behavior can look identical from the outside.
quickRule: Ask whether people are staying because the option was preselected or because change itself feels costly, risky, or abnormal.

left.label: Default effect
left.core: A preselected or no-action option receives extra uptake because the environment made it easiest.
left.ask: What happens if no option is preselected?

right.label: Status quo bias
right.core: People prefer the current state even when alternatives may be better.
right.ask: What objections appear when the current arrangement is treated as one option among many?

diagnosticQuestions: Is there an interface, form, policy, or enrollment architecture doing the steering?
Would active choice reduce the pattern?
Are people defending the current arrangement even when no default is present?

examples.situation: Most users keep the preselected privacy setting.
examples.why: Preselection is the immediate cause.

examples.situation: A committee resists replacing a familiar process despite evidence of better alternatives.
examples.why: The current arrangement feels safer because it is current.

repairMove: Run an active-choice version and ask the current option to win on forward-looking merits.

title: Availability Heuristic vs Frequency Illusion
summary: Availability mistakes ease of recall for prevalence; frequency illusion makes newly noticed things seem suddenly common.
commonConfusion: Both make something feel common because it is mentally easy to notice or retrieve.
quickRule: Ask whether the item is easy to recall because it is vivid, or newly salient because attention has been tuned to it.

left.label: Availability heuristic
left.core: Judgment uses mental availability as a shortcut for likelihood or frequency.
left.ask: Is the example memorable, recent, emotional, or heavily covered?

right.label: Frequency illusion
right.core: After noticing something, people start detecting it everywhere and infer that it has become more frequent.
right.ask: Did attention change before the perceived frequency changed?

diagnosticQuestions: Was the case already salient because it was vivid or emotionally loaded?
Did a new label, purchase, diagnosis, or conversation tune attention to the pattern?
What baseline count would distinguish real increase from noticing increase?

examples.situation: After dramatic plane-crash coverage, flying feels more dangerous.
examples.why: Vivid recall is being used as a risk estimate.

examples.situation: After learning a new term, a student starts seeing it everywhere.
examples.why: Attention changed, making instances easier to notice.

repairMove: Count occurrences against a baseline period before deciding whether prevalence changed.

title: Survivorship Bias vs Base Rate Neglect
summary: Survivorship bias samples only visible winners; base-rate neglect ignores the background frequency needed to interpret a case.
commonConfusion: Both lose the denominator, but they lose it differently.
quickRule: Ask whether the missing information is failed cases from the sample or background rates for the whole inference.

left.label: Survivorship bias
left.core: Visible successes dominate because failures are absent from the evidence set.
left.ask: Who tried the same path and vanished from view?

right.label: Base rate neglect
right.core: A vivid case description overwhelms the prior probability or population rate.
right.ask: What is the rate before this specific evidence updates it?

diagnosticQuestions: Are we studying only the winners?
Do we have the full starting cohort?
What prior probability should frame the case-specific evidence?

examples.situation: Business advice is drawn only from companies that survived.
examples.why: Failed companies are missing from the sample.

examples.situation: A personality sketch overwhelms the fact that one role is far more common in the applicant pool.
examples.why: The background frequency is ignored during inference.

repairMove: Recover the full cohort first, then apply the relevant base rate before updating on case details.

title: Groupthink vs Social Desirability Bias
summary: Groupthink is a group decision dynamic that suppresses dissent; social desirability bias is distorted reporting caused by wanting to look acceptable.
commonConfusion: In meetings, people may privately disagree but publicly report agreement, so both can be active.
quickRule: Ask whether the main distortion is convergence in group deliberation or self-presentational reporting.

left.label: Groupthink
left.core: A group protects harmony, identity, or momentum at the expense of independent evaluation.
left.ask: What dissent would be socially expensive to voice?

right.label: Social desirability bias
right.core: People report what sounds acceptable, virtuous, loyal, or competent rather than what they actually think or do.
right.ask: What answer is socially rewarded in this setting?

diagnosticQuestions: Is the distortion happening during deliberation, measurement, or both?
Would anonymous responses differ from public comments?
Did the group protect agreement before reasons were tested?

examples.situation: A team avoids raising objections after a founder endorses a plan.
examples.why: Harmony is replacing independent evaluation.

examples.situation: Employees rate a disliked training highly on a nonanonymous survey.
examples.why: The reported opinion is shaped by acceptable self-presentation.

repairMove: Use anonymous first-pass judgments, then give dissent an explicit protected role in the discussion.

title: Illusion Of Explanatory Depth vs Dunning-Kruger Effect
summary: Illusion of explanatory depth is overestimating how well you understand a mechanism; Dunning-Kruger is miscalibrated self-assessment when limited skill hides what is missing.
commonConfusion: Both can produce confident beginners, especially when the learner has not yet faced a real performance test.
quickRule: Ask whether the missing piece is mechanism explanation or broader competence calibration.

left.label: Illusion of explanatory depth
left.core: People feel they understand how something works until asked to explain the causal steps.
left.ask: Can the person produce the mechanism without relying on recognition?

right.label: Dunning-Kruger effect
right.core: Low skill can come with inflated self-assessment because the person lacks the skill needed to notice gaps.
right.ask: How does self-rating compare with performance on representative tasks?

diagnosticQuestions: Is the test an explanation of a mechanism or a performance benchmark?
Does confidence drop when the person must explain steps?
Does confidence remain high despite poor task performance?

examples.situation: Someone cannot explain how a zipper works after saying it is simple.
examples.why: The failure appears when mechanism detail is requested.

examples.situation: A novice performs poorly but rates their skill near expert level.
examples.why: Performance and self-assessment are miscalibrated.

repairMove: Pair mechanism explanation with a representative task and compare confidence before and after feedback.

title: Loss Aversion vs Sunk Cost Effect
summary: Loss aversion overweights losses relative to gains; the sunk cost effect keeps investment going because prior costs feel like they must be redeemed.
commonConfusion: Continuing a bad project can feel like avoiding a loss, but the key question is whether past investment is doing the work.
quickRule: Ask whether the pain comes from possible future loss or from refusing to accept an unrecoverable past cost.

left.label: Loss aversion
left.core: Potential losses loom larger than comparable gains.
left.ask: Is the choice being dominated by what could be lost from here?

right.label: Sunk cost effect
right.core: Past investment keeps influencing a decision even though it cannot be recovered.
right.ask: Would we choose this again today if the past investment belonged to someone else?

diagnosticQuestions: Is the argument about future downside or past spending?
Would the decision change if previous costs were hidden?
What option has the best forward-looking expected value?

examples.situation: A team avoids a migration because giving up the old workflow feels like losing comfort and control.
examples.why: The feared loss is forward-looking.

examples.situation: A team funds a failing project because too much money has already been spent.
examples.why: Irrecoverable past investment is steering the decision.

repairMove: Run one pass that ignores past costs, then compare future gains and losses symmetrically.
